title: Chapter 7 — Dataset Bias and Fairness
objectives: Define dataset bias and fairness and explain why they affect trust and accountability
Distinguish sampling, measurement, historical, label, aggregation, and confirmation bias
Use distribution checks, correlations, disparate impact, and visualizations to detect bias
Explain ethical, social, legal, and business impacts of biased datasets
Compare procedural fairness, outcome fairness, equalized odds, calibration, and accuracy trade-offs
Select pre-processing, in-processing, and post-processing mitigation strategies
Apply best practices for fair data collection, labeling, validation, and auditing
Recognize how AIF360 and Fairlearn support fairness measurement and mitigation
Anticipate emerging fairness challenges in generative AI, federated learning, and regulation

parts.title: Introduction to bias and fairness
parts.bridge_in: Chapters 4 through 6 covered labels, cleaning, and exploratory analysis; this chapter asks whether datasets and models treat groups equitably. This opening clip defines dataset bias and fairness, explains why they matter for trust and accountability, and previews policy and audit examples that motivate the rest of the chapter.

parts.title: Types of dataset bias
parts.bridge_in: Once bias and fairness are defined, the next task is to name where bias enters the dataset. This clip distinguishes sampling, measurement, historical, label, aggregation, and confirmation bias so later detection and mitigation choices have a precise target.

parts.title: Detecting bias statistically
parts.bridge_in: Bias types are conceptual categories; audits need measurable evidence. This clip covers distribution comparison, statistical tests, proxy correlations, and disparate-impact checks, including the eighty-percent rule used in hiring contexts.
parts.split_reason: Section 7.3 is split: statistical detection and disparate impact are covered here; visualization and tools follow in part-04.

parts.title: Bias visualization and tools
parts.bridge_in: Numbers identify disparities, but stakeholders often need visual explanations. This clip uses dashboards, heatmaps, bar charts, and scatter plots to communicate bias screens, then introduces AI Fairness 360 and Fairlearn as toolkits for repeatable audits.
parts.split_reason: Continuation of sec:7.3 after statistical detection.

parts.title: Impacts of dataset bias
parts.bridge_in: Detection is not only a technical exercise; disparities create harms. This clip organizes bias impacts into ethical, social, legal, and business consequences, with COMPAS as a bridge case for understanding downstream accountability.

parts.title: Fairness definitions and trade-offs
parts.bridge_in: After seeing the impacts of bias, the next question is what it means for a system to be fair. This clip compares procedural and outcome fairness, explains accuracy-fairness trade-offs, and introduces equalized odds, calibration, transparency, accountability, and inclusivity.

parts.title: Mitigating bias in datasets
parts.bridge_in: Fairness definitions become actionable through interventions. This clip separates pre-processing, in-processing, and post-processing mitigation so teams can choose whether to change data, training objectives, or model outputs.

parts.title: Best practices for fairness
parts.bridge_in: Mitigation techniques are strongest when embedded in routine practice. This clip turns fairness into operating habits: representative collection, clear labeling guidelines, diverse annotators, regular audits, validation on diverse datasets, and impact assessments.

parts.title: Fairness toolkits and case study
parts.bridge_in: Best practices are easier to sustain when teams use repeatable tooling. This clip compares IBM AI Fairness 360 and Microsoft Fairlearn, then shows how gender bias in text data can be mitigated with debiased representations and ongoing evaluation.

parts.title: Emerging fairness topics
parts.bridge_in: The chapter closes by looking beyond current audits and mitigation recipes. This final clip surveys fairness challenges in generative AI, federated learning, evolving regulation, and real-world deployment, then connects the fairness lens to later modeling and governance chapters.
